title: Must the Goondas Act Be Made Even More Cruel?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: On 12 August 2014, the final day of the Assembly session, the AIADMK government hurriedly introduced and passed nineteen Bills without allowing time for detailed explanation or debate. One permits people accused of brewing and selling illicit liquor, drug offences, forest offences, sex-trade offences, sand smuggling and video piracy to be detained under the Goondas Act even for a first offence.

The existing law already permits detention of those committing such grave offences. The amendment replaces the requirement that a person be a habitual offender with the proposition that even a single offence may justify detention if it has a propensity to disturb public order.

This government has already used the Goondas Act to arrest and imprison opposition members and others it dislikes. It now gives itself the further convenience of invoking the law after one alleged offence. It is seeking another route to threaten, suppress, disable and imprison those it dislikes and those who democratically question its wrongdoing.

We have no objection to taking action against and punishing actual sexual offenders even for a first offence. But this government will fabricate accusations against innocent people, critics of AIADMK rule and those engaged in democratic opposition.

Writing about the amendment, *The Hindu* said: "If enacted, the amendments will mean that the principle that only habitual offenders will be detained under the Goondas Act will be changed to cover even a single offence, 'which has a propensity to disturb public order.'"

Former Madras High Court judge K. Chandru told the same newspaper: "The amendments will be violative of Article 22 of the Constitution, which prescribes the parameters for such detention laws." He has repeatedly noted that High Courts have flagged abuse of the Goondas Act and struck down numerous detentions. Removing the word "habitually," he cautioned, could permit still greater police misuse.

This law recalls white imperial rule: a person detained under it may be imprisoned for a year without trial. We know how it has been used under this government. We should especially remember what the courts have said about its actions.

Even before this new provision, soon after the AIADMK assumed office, a succession of DMK leaders was arrested: Veerapandi Arumugam and MLA J. Anbazhagan on 30 July 2011; Treasurer M. K. Stalin and Poondi Kalaivanan at Tiruvarur the same day; Purasai Renganathan on 2 August; N. K. K. P. Raja at Erode on 4 August; Tiruppur city deputy secretary T. K. T. Nagaraj on 6 August; MLA Anitha Radhakrishnan at Tiruchendur and the Thammampatti town secretary in Salem on 10 August; Pongalur Mani at Coimbatore on 17 August; former minister K. N. Nehru, Tiruchi city secretary Anbazhagan and Coimbatore city secretary Veeragopal on 25 August; MLA Soundarapandian at Lalgudi and former town-panchayat chairman A. C. Govindasamy at Kangeyam on 27 August; Gopinath, husband of a former Madurai Mayor, on 29 August; the Tiruchengode municipal chairman on 1 September; area secretary K. Dhanasekaran in Chennai and Jayaraman in Madurai on 9 September; Karuppasamy Pandian in Tirunelveli on 10 September; Minnalkodi in Madurai on 11 September; former minister Ponmudy in Villupuram on 18 September; MLA K. C. Palanisamy in Karur on 19 September; former minister K. P. P. Samy in Chennai on 23 September; K. P. P. Samy's brothers and Isari Ganesh on 2 November; and again former ministers Veerapandi Arumugam and I. Periasamy and Madurai district secretary Thalapathi on 3 June 2012.

The wicked intention was to prevent DMK front-rankers from moving freely. Even after courts granted bail, fresh false complaints were obtained and cases filed. When bail was again secured under law, some were detained under the Goondas Act without trial. With no other course left, the DMK Executive Committee unanimously condemned this vindictive jungle rule, joined it with the people's issues, and announced and properly conducted the fill-the-prisons agitation.

Police registered a case against the late Pottu Suresh, alleging that he obstructed officers at a vehicle checkpoint on the Tirumangalam-Madurai road and abused them. They registered another case on a land-grabbing complaint from Amarnath of Anna Nagar, Madurai. When the AIADMK came to power, it ordered his Goondas Act detention on those cases. R. L. Ashokan of Madurai challenged the order. On 12 November 2011, Justices C. Nagappan and T. Sudhanthiram quashed it because the detaining authority had not been shown sufficient material on which to reach the required satisfaction.

In a Madurai Bench case concerning K. Subramani of Virudhunagar, Justices K. Suguna and R. Mala observed that Goondas Act detention can imprison a person for a year without trial; authorities must carefully examine the grounds and cannot sign mechanically, damaging both the law's true purpose and individual liberty. Finding that the District Collector had signed without examining the evidence, they quashed the order and fined both the Collector and Inspector Guru Venkat Raj Rs 5,000 each, payable to Subramani.

Jayalalithaa's government also deliberately detained brother Veerapandi Arumugam, Salem district secretary and a senior DMK pioneer, under the Act. In their judgment of 13 October 2012, Justices K. N. Basha and Paul Vasanthakumar held that using the Goondas Act on the basis of land-grabbing cases violated Supreme Court rulings. None of the required procedures had been followed. Documents had not been supplied within five days of detention. The six cases from incidents in 2007-08 were legally required to be connected, but were not. Nor did the alleged acts disturb public order.

The judgment said the Salem Police Commissioner acted mechanically and in haste, without fully reading the records, solely to impose preventive detention, completely denying the personal liberty guaranteed by Article 22(5). The government acted with the same haste. Veerapandi Arumugam was not directly connected with any cited case. The Commissioner had acted with an ulterior motive and included false information. The court asked how officials could have examined about 500 pages in two days, declared the actions unlawful, and quashed the detention. Could there be a stronger condemnation of Jayalalithaa's government?

Hundreds detained under the Act had petitions pending for release. Acting Chief Justice Satish Kumar Agnihotri directed that they be decided swiftly, and a special Division Bench was created. On 14 June 2014, Justices Dhanapalan and Chockalingam began at 10:30 a.m., heard 220 cases by 2 p.m., and delivered orders in 212, releasing those 212 detainees.

On 5 August 2013, *The Indian Express* wrote: "While there is concern over history-sheeters detained under the Goondas Act walking free using legal loop-holes, human rights advocates are equally worried over the frequent political abuse of the legislation. They say the stringent legislation, whose objective is to contain anti-social elements, has become a political weapon to settle scores."

It also wrote: "Over the years, the Goondas Act has become a toothless law thanks to the sloppy enforcement by the bureaucracy and police. So much so that over 80 per cent of those detained under the Goondas Act manage to walk out of jail within a few months either on the orders of the judiciary or Advisory Board, thereby reducing the invocation of the Act to a mere ritual. Curiously, in most cases, the detentions are quashed on the grounds of 'non-application of mind' by the officials/officers concerned while passing the detention orders. The Madras High Court has time and again quashed the detentions due to the silly mistakes of the officials."
This shows how thoughtlessly and recklessly the law is enforced.

But like adding a double lock to Ottakoothar's song, the AIADMK government has made the law harsher and more cruel. It has relaxed the rule that detention is for repeat offenders so that a single alleged offence will suffice. On behalf of the DMK, I declare that this extremely dangerous amendment, passed with the malicious intention of enabling political revenge against opposition members, is an attempt to dig a deep pit and bury democracy so that it can never rise again.
With affection,
M. K.
15 August 2014
